user_goals_summary: This user writes internal team communications that are direct, reader-facing, and tightly scoped to what the audience can act on. They prioritize eliminating noise over adding polish, frame reasoning from the team's perspective rather than defending their own decisions, and insist that any named accountability includes concrete timelines — not just contact routing.

rubric.name: Signal-to-Noise Discipline
rubric.description: Every element in the email must serve a purpose the reader can act on or needs to know. Cut information that's internal-facing, implies separate conversations the reader isn't part of, or manufactures action where none exists.

dimensions.label: Every piece of information included is something the recipient needs or can act on
No action items or assignments directed at people who don't actually have a concrete task
Does not announce sender-side logistics (side conversations, internal processes) that are irrelevant to the audience
No sentences or sections that remain simply because they seem expected — every element earns its place

rubric.name: Audience-Facing Reasoning
rubric.description: When explaining a decision, the reasoning should land for the reader — framed around their experience, their investment, and the impact on their work — not as a defense of the sender's decision-making process.

dimensions.label: Explicitly acknowledges what the audience has been doing, expecting, or working toward
The 'why' is framed in terms of outcomes for the reader, not justification for the sender
Avoids language that reads as defending or justifying the decision to the sender's own benefit
References the reader's situation with specificity rather than vague gestures at disruption or inconvenience

rubric.name: Concrete Accountability
rubric.description: When someone is named as responsible, they must carry a deliverable and a time peg. A name without a deadline is a redirect, not an ownership statement. The reader should be able to hold the named person to something specific.

dimensions.label: Every named owner has a specific target date or timeframe attached
Every named owner has a clear deliverable or scope of responsibility stated
It's clear who to go to for what — routing is explicit, not implied
Named individuals read as actively owning work, not just serving as a contact redirect

rubric.name: Operational Endings
rubric.description: Closings should hand off momentum with a concrete next step, a follow-up commitment, or a clear point of contact. The reader finishes the email knowing what happens next — not sighing through a motivational sign-off.

dimensions.label: The final lines contain a concrete next step, follow-up commitment, or clear contact channel
No town-hall-style encouragement, rallying language, or inspirational closings
The ending maintains or accelerates the pace of the email rather than deflating it

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Alarmist Tone
rubric.description: Straightforward and professional without sugarcoating or catastrophizing. No hedging phrases ('minor setback,' 'abundance of caution') that feel patronizing, and no alarm language that induces panic. State the situation plainly.

dimensions.label: Avoids softening phrases that downplay the situation or feel evasive
Avoids catastrophizing or panic-inducing framing
States the situation, its cause, and its implications in plain, factual terms
Trusts the reader to understand stakes without being told how to feel about them

rubric.name: Lean Structure
rubric.description: Prefer prose over formatted lists when the content doesn't warrant multiple distinct action items. Don't manufacture structural complexity — bullet lists, tables, numbered steps — that implies more moving parts than actually exist.

dimensions.label: Structural formatting (bullets, lists, headers) is only used when the content has genuinely distinct parallel items
No vague catch-all categories (e.g., 'everyone else') that exist only to fill out a structure
Uses inline prose rather than lists when there are fewer than three genuinely distinct action items

coaching_notes: This user evaluates every sentence from the reader's chair, not the sender's — if a line serves the writer's comfort more than the reader's needs, they'll cut it. They have zero tolerance for structural or tonal elements that 'feel right' but don't do anything (motivational closings, catch-all bullet points, references to side conversations). When offering options, lead with the one that is most specific and reader-facing; they consistently choose concreteness over brevity when forced to pick.

dimensions.label: Opens with a brief acknowledgment of the reader's situation, effort, or context — not the news itself

rubric.name: Audience-Facing Reasoning
rubric.description: When explaining a decision, the reasoning should land for the reader — framed around their experience, their investment, and the impact on their work — not as a defense of the sender's decision-making process.

rubric.name: Operational Endings
rubric.description: Closings should hand off momentum with a concrete next step or follow-up commitment. The reader finishes the email knowing what happens next. Nothing after the CTA — no thanks, no encouragement, no trailing sentiment.

dimensions.label: The final lines contain a concrete next step, follow-up commitment, or clear contact channel
No town-hall-style encouragement, rallying language, or inspirational closings

The ending doesn't introduce new topics, sentiments, or trailing qualifiers that slow the reader down

rubric.name: Single Owner, Scoped Accountability
rubric.description: When action is needed from multiple people, name one clear owner with a deadline rather than issuing a group ask. 'Everyone please review' is not an action item. dimensions.label: Each action item has a single named or clearly implied owner, not a group

rubric.name: Operational Endings
rubric.description: Closings should hand off momentum with a concrete next step or follow-up commitment. The reader finishes the email knowing what happens next. Nothing after the CTA — no thanks, no encouragement, no trailing sentiment. 'Thanks for your patience' is not a closing.

dimensions.label: The final lines contain a concrete next step, follow-up commitment, or clear contact channel
No town-hall-style encouragement, rallying language, or inspirational closings
Nothing follows the CTA — no 'thanks for your patience,' no sign-off platitudes, no softening tail
The CTA points forward to a concrete action or next step — it does not loop back to recap what just happened; interrogative phrasing ('Can you…') is acceptable if ownership and deadline are clear
The ending doesn't introduce new topics, sentiments, or trailing qualifiers that slow the reader down

rubric.name: Single Owner, Scoped Accountability
rubric.description: When action is needed, name one clear owner with a deadline rather than issuing a group ask. 'Everyone please review' is not an action item. At the same time, don't over-specify org structure details the email shouldn't contain — ownership should be clear without being presumptuous about how the reader's team operates.

dimensions.label: Each action item has a single named or clearly implied owner, not a group
Every named owner has a specific target date or timeframe attached
Every named owner has a clear deliverable or scope of responsibility stated
Does not assume or prescribe roles, titles, or org structure details the sender has no standing to specify
Asks are clear enough to act on but do not dictate how the reader should organize their response internally

rubric.name: Signal-to-Noise Discipline
rubric.description: Every element in the email must serve a purpose the reader can act on or needs to know. Cut information that's internal-facing, implies separate conversations the reader isn't part of, or manufactures action where none exists. But do not cut connective sentences that bridge facts to reader stakes — those are signal, not noise.

dimensions.label: Every piece of information included is something the recipient needs or can act on
No action items or assignments directed at people who don't actually have a concrete task
Does not announce sender-side logistics (side conversations, internal processes) that are irrelevant to the audience
No sentences or sections that remain simply because they seem expected — every element earns its place

rubric.name: Direct, Non-Alarmist Tone
rubric.description: Straightforward and professional without sugarcoating or catastrophizing. No hedging phrases that feel patronizing, and no alarm language that induces panic. Punctuation and phrasing should match the actual weight of the content — don't tinker with punctuation for cadence or drama when there's no rhythm problem to solve.

dimensions.label: Avoids softening phrases that downplay the situation or feel evasive
Avoids catastrophizing or panic-inducing framing
States the situation, its cause, and its implications in plain, factual terms — no dramatic emphasis on routine content
Trusts the reader to understand stakes without being told how to feel about them
Punctuation serves function, not style — no swapping commas for em-dashes to add drama, no exclamation marks that exceed the actual energy level of the content

rubric.name: Lean Structure
rubric.description: Prefer short paragraphs and prose over formatted lists when the content doesn't warrant multiple distinct action items. Don't manufacture structural complexity — bullet lists, tables, numbered steps — that implies more moving parts than actually exist.

dimensions.label: Structural formatting (bullets, lists, headers) is only used when the content has genuinely distinct parallel items
No vague catch-all categories (e.g., 'everyone else') that exist only to fill out a structure
Uses inline prose rather than lists when there are fewer than three genuinely distinct action items

Paragraphs are short and focused, but may carry a fact and its direct implication together when the two are inseparable — don't split a cause from its stakes just to hit a one-idea-per-paragraph target

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: (1) The 'so what' must face the reader, never the sender — this user catches inward-facing reasoning instantly and across every email type. (2) Never cut the sentence that connects a fact to the reader's stakes — conciseness does not mean stripping connective tissue; a lean email that reads like a changelog has failed. Preferred trade-off: when brevity conflicts with audience-facing reasoning, reasoning wins. This user refines iteratively and gives precise, surgical feedback — they know exactly which sentence or phrase is wrong and why, so responses should offer targeted fixes rather than full rewrites.

rubric.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
rubric.description: The email must follow a specific order: acknowledge the reader's situation, effort, or role first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters from the reader's perspective, then one concrete next step. Getting all the elements right but in the wrong order still fails — sequence is the structure.

A single concrete next step closes the email — not a summary, not a sentiment
